claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 10 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 2 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 2 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation

expected_eligibility.name: Blake

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To begin, I need to ask you about the child you're claiming for.

**How old is your child?**

Please let me know which category applies:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

This helps me determine which eligibility rules apply to your situation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm actually asking about two children - I have Alex who's 10 years old, and Blake who's 2. So they're both under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for letting me know about both of your children! Since both Alex (10 years old) and Blake (2 years old) are under 16, they both automatically qualify for the age requirement. Let me continue the assessment for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Are both Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Given their ages (10 and 2), I'd expect neither of them is working, but I just need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work at all - they're far too young for that!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes perfect sense given their ages. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Are both Alex and Blake NOT receiving any benefits in their own right?**

This means they are not personally claiming benefits such as:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

Again, given their young ages (10 and 2), this would be very unusual, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - neither of them receives any benefits in their own name. They're just kids.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Exactly, that's what I'd expect! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT in any apprenticeships?**

Given their ages (10 and 2), I'm confident they won't be, but I need to confirm. For reference, certain apprenticeships would disqualify a child from Child Benefit (though Foundation Apprenticeships in Wales are an exception).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not - they're way too young to be in any kind of apprenticeship!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely, that's what I expected! Let me move on to questions about your situation as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status.

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You only need to meet **one** of these to qualify:

1. **Normally live in the UK** - You usually reside in the UK
2. **Settled status** - You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Pre-settled status and working** - You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3+ continuous months)
4. **Pre-settled status and job seeking** - You have pre-settled status and are actively looking for work (can claim for up to 91 days)
5. **Pre-settled status with sufficient resources** - You have pre-settled status and can financially support yourself and family (without claiming certain benefits)
6. **Pre-settled status and studying** - You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. **Pre-settled status as family member** - You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **Crown servant abroad** - You're living abroad as a Crown servant

Which of these applies to you? (The most common answer is simply that you normally live in the UK.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, I don't live in the UK at all - I'm living abroad at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Living abroad can sometimes still qualify you for Child Benefit under certain circumstances.

**Are you living abroad as a Crown servant?**

Crown servants include people like:
- Diplomats and embassy staff
- Armed forces personnel stationed overseas
- Certain government employees working abroad

Or are you living abroad in another capacity? If so, could you tell me a bit more about your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not a Crown servant - I'm just living abroad as a regular person, not working for the government or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Unfortunately, based on what you've told me, I need to deliver some news about your eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'm sorry, but based on the information you've provided, **you are not currently eligible for UK Child Benefit** for either Alex or Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Summary

| Child | Age | Eligible? | Reason |
|-------|-----|-----------|--------|
| **Alex** | 10 | ❌ No | Claimant doesn't meet UK residency requirements |
| **Blake** | 2 | ❌ No | Claimant doesn't meet UK residency requirements |

### Why?
Both Alex and Blake meet all the requirements for being qualifying children (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship). However, to claim Child Benefit, **you** as the claimant must meet UK residency requirements.

Since you're living abroad and are not a Crown servant or someone with settled/pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, you don't currently qualify.

### What You Can Do

1. **If your circumstances change** - If you return to live in the UK, you could then apply for Child Benefit.

2. **Check for other exceptions** - There may be specific circumstances or reciprocal agreements with certain countries. You can contact HMRC directly for advice on your specific situation.

3. **Contact HMRC** - If you think there might be something unique about your situation, you can reach out to the Child Benefit Office:
   - Website: [gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit)
   - Phone: 0300 200 3100

I'm sorry I couldn't bring better news. Is there anything else you'd like to know or clarify?

reasonings: Alex (10 years old) meets all the child-related criteria: under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, and not in an apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet UK residency requirements - they are living abroad and are not a Crown servant, nor do they have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme. Therefore, the claimant cannot receive Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake (2 years old) meets all the child-related criteria: under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, and not in an apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet UK residency requirements - they are living abroad and are not a Crown servant, nor do they have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme. Therefore, the claimant cannot receive Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: While both children (Alex, age 10, and Blake, age 2) fully meet the child eligibility criteria for Child Benefit, the claimant does not satisfy the UK residency requirements. The claimant is living abroad as a regular person, not as a Crown servant, and does not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme. UK residency (or meeting specific exceptions) is a fundamental requirement for receiving Child Benefit.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.

Blake (2 years old) meets all the child-related criteria: under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, and not in an apprenticeship. Therefore, the claimant cannot receive Child Benefit for Blake.
